 India has the second largest rail network in the world. That, combined with the fact that 23 million passengers a day ride its rails, help to explain why its trains are so overcrowded that you can often see passengers hanging out the side. 
 It's a long-standing tradition to take the train in India — the oldest train in the world, built in 1855, also runs on the India rail network between New Delhi and Rajasthan. 
 If you think your commute is bad, or airport lines are giving you the travel blues, just take a look at these insanely crowded trains in India.

 Around 25,000 people die every year on India's railways. 
 Some of these casualties come from train wrecks, of which there are many, but the vast majority come from people falling out of open doors or being hit on the tracks, according to Agence France Presse. 
 During rush hours, trains are a nightmare in first class and general compartments, as commuters scramble for every inch of space. Bent on saving time, people recklessly cross tracks to catch their trains, and women often take to chopping vegetables in preparation for dinner.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2014-07-08
[TITLE]: #MODIFyingIndia : Getting the Indian Railway Back on Track #Budget2014-15 | MODIfying India
[METADATA/INTRO]: #MODIFyingIndia : Getting the Indian Railway Back on Track #Budget2014-15 It would be pertinent to know few facts about Indian Railway, which demonstrate how massive is the size of its operation....
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: #MODIFyingIndia : Getting the Indian Railway Back on Track #Budget2014-15 
 It would be pertinent to know few facts about Indian Railway, which demonstrate how massive is the size of its operation. 
 The Indian Railways carry 23 million passengers a day, annually Indian Railways ferries 7.2 billion passenger (approximately the total population of the world today); over 1 billion tonnes of freight every year; has the capacity to move the entire population of Australia; currently at No.3 biggest – Targeting to become the largest freight carrier in the world. 
 Indian Railways is one of the world’s largest railway networks with 115,000 kms of track over a route of 65,000 kms and 7,500 stations; it operates 11,000 trains everyday through 1.4 million employees. Indian Railways owns the longest railway platform in the world at Kharagpur (West Bengal) with a length of 2,733 ft (1072.5 m) in length.
